search_text: 392AC87D__Elk Hunt Packing List 2024.pdf other 2024 other_hunt        WILD EYEZ OUTFITTERS (UTAH) BIG GAME HUNT  SUGGESTED PACKING LIST AND HUNT OUTLINE      Hello, I am Tyler Miller of Wild Eyez Outfitters. This document is a generalized  packing list, preparation guide and hunt outline including safety tips, equipment  recommendations, and clothing suggestions for Big Game Hunting in Utah. If you  have been referred to this document, you may be considering or have contracted a  hunt with us, and we want to both thank you for that and congratulate you on your  upcoming hunting adventure. The items listed are as to client discretion for necessity  and they are also not brand specific. I may make brand recommendations and/or  whether a particular item is optional vs. essential. The ultimate selection is at each  client's discretion.  Weather conditions, terrain and elk habitat vary greatly in Utah. Change to general pheasant hunt: This  year, Utah will hold a single, statewide general  pheasant hunt on both public and private  lands. The hunt will run from Nov. 4–Dec. 3,  2017. For details, see the hunt table on page  36. New HIP registration process: Utah  now has an easier mobile-friendly process for  registering in the Migratory Game Bird Harvest  Information Program (HIP). This registration is  mandatory if you plan to hunt migratory game  birds—including American crow, band-tailed  pigeon, mourning dove, sandhill crane or  white-winged dove—during the 2017–2018  season. To register, visit wildlife.utah.gov/uthip.  For more information, please see page 9. Lower cost for resident youth general- season turkey permits: Starting this year,  a resident youth general-season turkey permit  will only cost $25. See all license and permit  fees on pages 6–7.

More fall turkey permits: Starting this  year, you can obtain up to three wild turkey  permits for the fall general-season hunt. (Each  permit is valid for one bird.) Shotgun change for turkey hunting:  While hunting wild turkeys, hunters may now  use any shotgun firing shot BB or smaller  diameter.

search_text: 5BE3A5CC__An Act relating to wildlife regulation.pdf other 2026 regulation  This is an Act requesting amendments to hb 168 before it proceeds further through legislation.  It  relates to wildlife regulation; amending Title 23A, Chapter 4, Part 12; clarifying regulatory  authority; creating the Utah Outfitter and Guide Board; establishing professional qualification  standards; providing for adjudicative review; requiring federal permit documentation for  outfitters operating on federal land; requiring public registry publication; and making technical  conforming amendments.  Section 1. Amend Title 23A, Chapter 4, Part  12.  23A-4-1202. Definitions.  For purposes of this part:  (1) “Division” means the Division of Wildlife Resources acting in its regulatory capacity. (2) “Board” means the Utah Outfitter and Guide Board created in Section 23A-4-1204.5 (3) “Compensation” means anything of value, direct or indirect, monetary or nonmonetary, received or expected in exchange for outfitting or guiding services. The DWR is seeking the public's feedback  on the recommendations. New rules for outfitters, guides and spotters A new law was passed during the 2025 legislative session to allow the Utah Department of Natural  Resources to regulate hunting guides and outfitters in the state. Previously, guides and outfitters  were regulated by the Utah Division of Professional Licensing. D176FC55__Elk Hunt Packing List 2024 (1).pdf other 2024 other_hunt        WILD EYEZ OUTFITTERS (UTAH) BIG GAME HUNT  SUGGESTED PACKING LIST AND HUNT OUTLINE      Hello, I am Tyler Miller of Wild Eyez Outfitters. This document is a generalized  packing list, preparation guide and hunt outline including safety tips, equipment  recommendations, and clothing suggestions for Big Game Hunting in Utah. search_text: 672FAE79__Predictive Engine for Hunts.pdf other 2026 harvest_report  Predictive Engine for Hunts Executive summary Building a predictive hunting engine is feasible, but it will only be as credible as its data provenance, bias controls, and  validation design. The fastest path to a trustworthy prototype is a  hybrid system: (a) a rigorous geospatial constraints layer (public access, legal seasons/units, closures), (b) a habitat–weather opportunity model that outputs calibrated probabilities on a spatial grid, and (c) a  user feedback loop that steadily improves labels and personalization while protecting privacy and avoiding wildlife disturbance. Assumptions (explicit because your scope is open-ended): - The prototype targets legal hunting decision support (not enforcement evasion), with  species-agnostic core plus species-specific “plug-ins” once you pick targets and regions. - The initial build focuses on  North America/US-first data availability, while keeping a path to global expansion using global datasets (e.g., ERA5, WorldCover , Copernicus DEM, W
